summary: Gruber traces the development of reports that Facebook is secretly building a phone, starting with Mike Arrington's TechCrunch scoop and followed by corroborating reports from multiple outlets. He notes Facebook's carefully worded denial that they're 'building a phone' while evidence suggests they're developing Android-based software for a branded device. Gruber corrects Arrington's comparison of Facebook's approach to Apple's, noting it's more like Google's Nexus One model. He speculates that deep Facebook integration and single sign-in could make sense, and that Facebook may want to position itself as a mobile platform peer to Apple, Google, and Microsoft rather than just another app. He concludes that with three publications backing the story and a narrow denial from Facebook, there's substantial evidence something is happening.
key_points: Facebook's denial was narrowly worded around 'building a phone' while evidence points to them developing software for a branded Android device built by a third party like Samsung or HTC
Gruber corrects the common conflation of Apple's hardware-software integration with the contract manufacturing model Facebook would use, which is closer to Google's Nexus One approach
The hiring of Joe Hewitt (Facebook iPhone app creator) and Matthew Papakipos (former Chrome OS lead from Google) suggests serious mobile ambitions
Facebook's strategic motivation may be to become a mobile platform peer with its own app store rather than remaining one app among thousands on others' platforms
Facebook's role on such a phone would mirror iTunes's role in iOS — deep system-level integration rather than a standalone app
money_quotes: That's actually not what Apple does. Apple does its own software and hardware engineering; it only farms out manufacturing.
But the only thing they really denied was that they were 'building a phone'. That's what Arrington's headline claimed, but it's not what his story actually alleged.
Facebook's role on such a phone would be like iTunes's role in iOS, and Google's services — Gmail, contacts, calendars, etc. — in Android.
This means that Google is now essentially helping Facebook attempt to destroy Google!

Could be that Facebook, as a company, doesn't want to be seen as one app among thousands in mobile app stores from Apple, Google, and Microsoft, but rather as a peer to those companies — a mobile titan with its own app store.
The whole story could be bullshit, but I'm a big believer in the tenet that where there's smoke, there's fire.
key_insight: Facebook's carefully worded denial and multiple corroborating reports suggest the company is pursuing a branded Android phone to elevate itself from app-among-thousands to mobile platform peer alongside Apple, Google, and Microsoft.
